topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating monotony, mirroring the dull ache behind Elias’s eyes. He stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers swimming before his gaze, each figure a tiny, sharp shard of unwelcome realization. It was Sarah’s salary, a figure that had just materialized on his screen, a stark contrast to his own meager earnings. A slow, cold knot tightened in his chest, settling deep beneath his ribs. He picked up his lukewarm coffee mug, the ceramic feeling heavy and unresponsive in his hand. He didn't look up when Sarah approached, her bright smile a jarring intrusion into his internal quiet. "Morning, Elias," she chirped, oblivious to the shadow that had just fallen across his face. He managed a tight, almost imperceptible nod, the silence between them thick with unspoken comparison.

[story 2]

The rain lashed against the skyscraper windows, each drop sounding like a tiny, insistent drumbeat against the glass. Mark leaned back in his chair, the leather groaning beneath him, and watched the cursor blink relentlessly on his monitor. The data had been undeniable, presented with the cold, hard clarity of mathematics. He felt a strange numbness spreading through his limbs, a heavy weight pressing him into his seat. He traced the edge of his desk with a fingertip, the wood cool beneath his touch. When Chloe finally walked past, carrying a stack of files, Mark didn't bother to look up. His jaw was set so tightly he could feel the muscle twitching beneath his skin. "Rough night?" she asked, her voice light and airy. He just exhaled slowly, a breath that seemed too deep for the space he occupied, and focused intently on a smudge on the screen.

[story 3]

Inside the cramped cubicle, the air felt stagnant, thick with the scent of old paper and stale air conditioning. David meticulously re-read the performance review, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. His hands, resting on the keyboard, were clenched so tightly his knuckles were white. The realization hit him not with a sudden shock, but with a slow, creeping erosion of his composure. He felt a profound sense of unfairness, a bitter taste rising in his throat. When Maria finally tapped on his desk, asking about the quarterly projections, David simply met her gaze. His eyes were flat, distant, holding a depth of unhappiness that made her pause. "Everything alright, David?" she inquired, her tone laced with concern, but he offered no reply, only the unwavering stillness of someone carrying a significant, unwelcome burden.
